scouted.category: Tiny text LLMs that generate language

candidates.name: delphi-suite/v0-llama2-12.8m (TinyStories Llama-2, 4096-vocab)
candidates.what_it_does: Writes coherent multi-sentence children's stories with near-perfect grammar (TinyStories regime). The genuine 'a cassette that writes stories' flex in the 3-20 MB band: small enough to fit, large enough to be qualitatively beyond babble.
candidates.params_or_nature: 14.56M params (verified: 58.2MB fp32 .bin / 4 = 14.55M, matches arch count to 0.1%)
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: Llama d=384, L=8, vocab=4096, inter=1024, TIED embeddings, GQA 16/8. Embeddings only 11% (tiny 4096 SentencePiece vocab is the unlock). int4 = 14.56M*0.5 = 7.28MB raw weights; lzma ~0.92x + 65KB tokenizer.model + ~20KB llama2.c-style runtime => ~6.7MB on tape. int8 variant = ~13.4MB.
candidates.runnable: Yes. Llama-2 arch runs on a single-file karpathy llama2.c engine (~20KB WASM); the repo already bundles a karpathy stories runtime for the sub-MB tier, so the inference path is proven. SentencePiece 4096 tokenizer is 64.5KB.
candidates.notes: Best in band. License nuance: HF model repos carry a `license:mit` metadata TAG (consistent across the whole delphi family: 6.4m/12.8m/25.6m all tagged MIT), but there is NO LICENSE file in the model repo. The delphi CODE repo (github.com/delphi-suite/delphi) is verified Apache-2.0. Either way permissive. Marked probable (not verified) because no LICENSE file ships in the weight repo. The 4096-vocab SentencePiece is what makes story models fit a cassette - 50K-vocab GPT-Neo wastes 70-80% of bytes on embeddings.

candidates.name: delphi-suite/v0-llama2-25.6m (TinyStories Llama-2, 4096-vocab)
candidates.what_it_does: Larger TinyStories model - better story coherence/consistency than the 12.8m, still tiny vocab. The 'fill more of the tape, get better prose' upper-band story option.
candidates.params_or_nature: 25.31M params (arch-derived; same family/method as 12.8m which validated to 0.1%)
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: Llama d=512, L=8, vocab=4096, inter=1376, TIED, GQA 16/8 => 25.31M params (emb only 8%). int4 = 12.65MB raw; lzma ~0.92x + 65KB tokenizer + 20KB runtime => ~11.7MB on tape. int8 = ~23.3MB (just over a C90-stereo-15kbps tape).
candidates.runnable: Yes - identical runtime story to the 12.8m (llama2.c single-file engine, 4096 SentencePiece tokenizer).
candidates.notes: Same license caveat as the 12.8m (MIT metadata tag, no LICENSE file in weight repo; code repo Apache-2.0). int4 fits the 8-15 MB electrical-stereo tier comfortably; int8 is the stretch-tier (20-40MB dream) target for maximum story quality. Sweet spot when the bitrate climbs.

candidates.name: roneneldan/TinyStories-8M (GPT-Neo, the canonical TinyStories family)
candidates.what_it_does: The famous TinyStories model line that PROVED sub-GPT-2 models can write fluent, grammatical, multi-paragraph stories (arXiv 2305.07759). The 8M is the named non-embedding size; writes coherent kids' stories.
candidates.params_or_nature: 32.55M total params (8M is the non-embedding count; the 50K GPT-Neo vocab adds 26.3M embedding params = 81% of total)
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: GPT-Neo d=256, L=8, vocab=50257, inter=1024, UNtied + 2048 learned pos. Total 32.55M (81% is the wasteful 50K vocab). int4 = 16.28MB + 3.36MB GPT-2 BPE tokenizer => ~16.7MB on tape AS-IS. KEY LEVER: vocab-trim to ~8192 (stories use few tokens) drops it to ~11M params => int4 ~5.4MB on tape.
candidates.runnable: Yes - GPT-Neo arch runs on a small transformers/GGML loop; but the 50K GPT-2 BPE tokenizer (3.4MB) is heavy vs the 65KB SentencePiece of the delphi models.
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND on license grounds: roneneldan/TinyStories-* MODEL repos have NO LICENSE file and NO license metadata tag (confirmed - no `license:` in repo tags). Web claims of 'MIT' conflate it with... nothing verifiable; the DATASET is CDLA-Sharing-1.0, a different artifact. The repo's own prior research already flagged this exact repo as 'NO license declared.' Use the MIT-tagged delphi models instead for the same story-writing flex. Listed to make the gap visible - and because vocab-trimming a 50K->8K model is the technique that makes 50K-vocab models fit at all.

candidates.name: EleutherAI/pythia-70m (Apache-2.0 general LM)
candidates.what_it_does: A genuine general-purpose autoregressive LM (Pile-trained, not story-specialized). At 70M it BABBLES - produces fluent-looking but largely incoherent text. Value is the clean Apache-2.0 license + being a real general LM, not a toy.
candidates.params_or_nature: 70.4M params (HF reports 95.6M counting the untied 50K in+out embeddings; real .bin is 166MB fp16... actually fp16-stored, 70M body)
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: GPT-NeoX d=512, L=6, vocab=50304, inter=2048, UNtied, RoPE => 70.39M params (73% is the 50K vocab). int4 = 35.2MB raw + 2.1MB tokenizer => ~35MB on tape AS-IS (OVER 20MB band). Fits only via vocab-trim or int4+embedding-ternary. fp16 file is 166MB.
candidates.runnable: Yes - GPT-NeoX runs on llama.cpp/transformers. But 50K-vocab + untied embeddings make it bulky for what it delivers (babble).
candidates.notes: License VERIFIED apache-2.0 (explicit metadata + Pile/Pythia papers). NOT recommended: at 70M general-domain it babbles (the TinyStories paper itself shows ~125M general models 'rarely generate coherent text beyond a few words'), AND int4 ~35MB is over the 20MB band. Only fits with aggressive 50K->~10K vocab trim (-> ~25M params, int4 ~13MB). Keep as the clean-license general-LM reference; story-specialized delphi models dominate it on both size and coherence.

candidates.name: EleutherAI/pythia-160m (Apache-2.0 general LM)
candidates.what_it_does: Larger Pile-trained general LM. Still not reliably coherent prose, but the embedding fraction drops (48%) so it's a more 'real' transformer per byte. Apache-2.0, well-documented (Pythia suite).
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: GPT-NeoX d=768, L=12, vocab=50304, inter=3072, UNtied, RoPE => 162.2M params. int4 = 81.1MB raw - WELL OVER even the 40MB dream tier. Listed for completeness/license-clarity, not size-fit.
candidates.runnable: Yes (llama.cpp/transformers), but does not fit any cassette tier.
candidates.notes: License VERIFIED apache-2.0. Out of band at every quant (int4 81MB). Included to bound the upper edge: anything >=125M params with a 50K vocab cannot fit even the 40MB dream tape at int4. The lesson the cassette enforces: SMALL VOCAB + STORY-SPECIALIZED beats BIG-VOCAB GENERAL every time on bytes-per-coherence.

candidates.name: HuggingFaceTB/SmolLM2-135M-Instruct (Apache-2.0, instruct/chat)
candidates.what_it_does: The genuinely IMPRESSIVE one if the bitrate ever climbs enough: a real instruction-tuned chat model that answers questions and follows prompts (not just stories). 'A cassette you can chat with.' Apache-2.0, 49K vocab but TIED embeddings.
candidates.params_or_nature: 134.52M params (verified arch count; real model.safetensors = 269MB fp16)
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: Llama d=576, L=30, vocab=49152, inter=1536, TIED, GQA 9/3 => 134.52M params (emb only 21% thanks to tying). int4 = 67.26MB raw + 2.1MB tokenizer => ~67MB on tape. OVER the 40MB dream tier even at int4. Only int2/ternary-QAT (~34MB) approaches the stretch tape.
candidates.runnable: Yes - Llama arch on llama.cpp; instruct chat template. The capability is real (basic Q&A, summarization, simple chat), unlike the babbling sub-100M general models.
candidates.notes: License VERIFIED apache-2.0. The WOW ceiling for this category - a chattable, instruction-following model - but int4 ~67MB does NOT fit even the 36MB dream tape. Flag as the 'aspirational top' target: a ternary/int2 QAT build (~34MB) would just squeeze the C120-stereo-20kbps dream tier and would be a category-defining flex ('a cassette you can have a conversation with'). Recommend revisiting if a <=2-bit QAT checkpoint with verified-permissive license appears. Base model (SmolLM2-135M) is identical size, also Apache-2.0.

candidates.name: openai-community/gpt2 (GPT-2 small, MIT)
candidates.what_it_does: The historic GPT-2 small - the model the TinyStories paper used as the COUNTEREXAMPLE (125M general model that 'rarely generates coherent text beyond a few words'). MIT-licensed, iconic. More a milestone artifact than a coherent generator.
candidates.size_basis: Real arch: GPT-2 d=768, L=12, vocab=50257, inter=3072, TIED, 1024 learned pos => 124.34M params. int4 = 62.17MB raw + 3.36MB BPE tokenizer => ~62MB on tape. OVER the dream tier. fp32 file is 548MB.
candidates.runnable: Yes - GPT-2 is the most-supported arch anywhere (llama.cpp, transformers.js, even pure-JS). Trivial to run; just doesn't fit a tape at useful quant.
candidates.notes: License VERIFIED mit (explicit metadata + DOI). Does not fit the band (int4 62MB). Listed because it's the canonical 'name everyone knows' and the rhetorical foil: GPT-2-small at 124M babbles, while delphi-12.8m at 14.5M writes coherent stories - the perfect cassette narrative ('the tape holds something SMARTER per byte than GPT-2'). Use GPT-2 in the writeup as contrast, ship a delphi model.

candidates.name: adamkarvonen Chess-GPT (lichess 8-layer, 25M)
candidates.what_it_does: A 25M-param nanoGPT trained on Lichess PGN that PLAYS REAL CHESS: 99.6% legal-move rate and ~46% win-rate vs Stockfish 16 level-0 (~1300 Elo). The famous 'Chess-GPT has an internal world model' model (COLM 2024) — linear probes recover a ~98%-accurate board state from its activations by layer 5. A dramatic upgrade over the 6.6M chess-gpt already on the shortlist: it actually beats a real engine instead of just emitting plausible moves.
candidates.size_basis: REAL: lichess_8layers_ckpt_no_optimizer.pt = 103 MB fp32 on HF -> 25.0M params (103MB/4B). int4 quant = 0.5 B/param = 12.5 MB. Tokenizer is a 32-char chess vocab (<1KB, like the local chess-gpt's vocab.json). nanoGPT inference runtime is a few KB of Python. lzma barely helps quantized weights (measured: xz only cut the local fp32 chess-gpt by 8%), so ~12.5 MB stands.
candidates.runnable: Yes — nanoGPT forward pass (PyTorch or a ~200-line numpy/C reimpl). No tokenizer dependency beyond a 32-char map. Same runtime class as the chess-gpt already staged in payloads/llm_over_budget/.
candidates.notes: STRONGEST FLEX of the category — a cassette that out-plays a chess engine. CAVEAT: the HF weights repo (adamkarvonen/chess_llms) has NO LICENSE file and empty YAML metadata. License is inferred PROBABLE because the training repo github.com/adamkarvonen/train_ChessGPT (a karpathy/nanoGPT fork) is VERIFIED MIT and the project README states 'all code, models, and datasets are open source.' Before shipping, confirm with the author or treat as MIT-via-training-repo. The repo also has a 6-layer ckpt (15.8MB fp32 = ~3.9M params, int4 ~2MB) and a 16-layer (204MB = ~50M params, int4 ~25MB) for other tiers.

candidates.name: blueapple8259 / TinyCode-python (30.4M)
candidates.what_it_does: A 30.4M-param LLaMA-architecture model trained on the Python subset of bigcode/starcoderdata — a cassette that AUTOCOMPLETES PYTHON CODE. Give it a function signature or a partial line and it continues plausible Python. The 'a tape that writes code' flex in the smallest credible form.
candidates.size_basis: REAL: model.safetensors = 122 MB fp32 on HF -> 30.4M params (matches card's stated 30.4M). int4 = 0.5 B/param = 15.2 MB. PLUS tokenizer overhead: tokenizer.json 2.16MB + vocab.json 817KB + merges.txt 462KB ~= 3.4MB raw, lzma-compresses to ~1.5-2MB (BPE merge tables are text, ~50% compressible). Total on-tape ~16.7-17.2 MB.
candidates.runnable: Yes — standard LLaMA forward pass (transformers / llama.cpp-class runtime), int4 GGUF works directly. Needs the BPE tokenizer shipped (counted above).
candidates.notes: License is 'mit' in HF metadata but there is NO LICENSE file in the repo -> PROBABLE not VERIFIED. Solidly in the 8-20 MB 'electrical/stereo climb' tier. The large GPT-2 BPE tokenizer is the hidden cost on tape — a smaller code model with a byte-level tokenizer would be more efficient, but this is the smallest verified-real Python coder found.

candidates.name: AlignmentResearch / learned-planner — Sokoban DRC(3,3) RNN
candidates.what_it_does: A recurrent ConvLSTM (DRC) agent that PLANS its way through Sokoban puzzles — the model from the ICML-2024 'Planning behavior in a recurrent neural network that plays Sokoban' paper. It demonstrably does internal forward-search/planning (give it more thinking-time ConvLSTM ticks and it solves harder boxes). A cassette that literally thinks ahead to push boxes onto goals. Includes a 3.07M ResNet variant for a bigger tier.
candidates.size_basis: REAL: DRC(3,3) checkpoint = 1,285,125 params (stated on the model card). int8 = 1 B/param = 1.29 MB; fp16 = 2.6 MB; int4 = 0.64 MB. No tokenizer (it consumes a Sokoban grid tensor, not text). Tiny ConvLSTM runtime. ResNet variant = 3,068,421 params (int4 ~1.5MB, int8 ~3.1MB) if a bigger payload is wanted.
candidates.runnable: Yes — small DRC/ConvLSTM forward pass; the AlignmentResearch/learned-planner repo (Apache-2.0) ships the exact inference code. Pure tensor ops, no tokenizer.
candidates.notes: LICENSE VERIFIED: Apache-2.0 declared on BOTH the HF model card AND the GitHub repo (which has a LICENSE file). The single best license story in this batch and a genuinely novel flex ('a tape that PLANS'). Small enough to fit a C60 acoustic side many times over — could pair with another payload. Highest-confidence recommendation.

candidates.name: Baidicoot / Othello-GPT (TransformerLens, ~25M)
candidates.what_it_does: The canonical Othello-GPT (Li et al. 'Emergent World Representations') — a GPT that predicts legal Othello/Reversi moves and was shown to build an internal model of the 8x8 board. The original interpretability-famous 'it has a world model' result. A cassette that plays Othello AND provably represents the board internally. 41.3K downloads — the most-used Othello-GPT on the Hub.
candidates.size_basis: REAL: final.pth = 101 MB fp32 on HF -> ~25.2M params (101MB/4B; consistent with the Li et al. 8-layer d=512 GPT). int4 = 0.5 B/param = 12.6 MB. No text tokenizer — vocab is 60 board squares (a <1KB int map). TransformerLens/nanoGPT runtime is a few KB.
candidates.runnable: Yes — GPT forward pass over a 61-token move vocab; trivial integer 'tokenizer'. Runs on the same nanoGPT-class runtime as the chess models.
candidates.notes: License 'mit' shown in HF header/tags but NO LICENSE file in the repo -> PROBABLE. The mntss/Othello-GPT mirror is also tagged MIT (also no LICENSE file). Strong, well-known model in the 8-20MB tier; verify license with author before shipping. Pairs thematically with Chess-GPT as a 'board-game world-model' double feature.

candidates.name: Q-bert / ChessGPT (87.5M) — STRETCH tier
candidates.what_it_does: An 87.5M-param GPT-2 chess model — the largest credible chess transformer that could plausibly fit the 20-40MB 'dream' (C120 stereo @20kbps) tier. A cassette holding a substantially stronger chess brain than the 25M model.
candidates.size_basis: REAL: model.safetensors = 350 MB fp32 on HF -> 87.5M params (matches card's 87.5M; 350MB/4B). int4 = 0.5 B/param = 43.75 MB; int3 ~32.8 MB. Tokenizer (tokenizer.json 342KB + added_tokens 29.5KB) ~ +0.2MB lzma. So int4 ~44MB (just over the 40MB dream ceiling); int3/aggressive-quant ~33MB squeaks into the C120-stereo-@20kbps tier.
candidates.runnable: Yes — GPT-2 forward pass; needs ~33-44MB depending on quant aggressiveness. int4 (44MB) overshoots; only fits the dream tier with int3 or 36MB@20kbps-C120.
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND for the 3-20MB target band: at int4 it is ~44MB, over even the 40MB dream ceiling. Listed so the upper-bound is visible. License 'mit' in metadata, no LICENSE file -> PROBABLE. The bigger nsarrazin/chessformer (232M, MIT, int4 ~116MB) and jd0g/chess-gpt (200M, GATED) are far over budget — noted for completeness, not viable on tape.

candidates.name: awonga / othello-gpt-7M
candidates.what_it_does: A 7M-param Othello-GPT reproduction — would be an attractive ~3.5MB acoustic-tier 'plays Othello' payload if the license were known.
candidates.size_basis: REAL: model.safetensors = 30.2 MB fp32 on HF -> ~7.05M params (30.2MB/4B; consistent with card's '7M'). int4 = 0.5 B/param = 3.5 MB. 60-square integer vocab, no text tokenizer.
candidates.runnable: Yes — small GPT forward pass over the Othello move vocab.
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND: NO license declared anywhere (verified: no LICENSE file, no metadata tag, model card is empty placeholders). Perfectly sized for the acoustic C90/C120 tier (~3.5MB int4) and would be a great pick IF licensed — flagged as the visible gap. The awonga repo also has othello-gpt-30l and othello-gpt-6M-b, all equally unlicensed.

candidates.name: edbeeching / decision_transformer_atari
candidates.what_it_does: The Hugging Face Decision-Transformer Atari checkpoints — a GPT-2-based RL agent that, conditioned on a target return, plays Atari games (Breakout, Pong, Qbert, Seaquest) by autoregressive action prediction. 'A cassette that plays Atari' would be a top-tier flex.
candidates.size_basis: ESTIMATE from repo: checkpoints/ folder = ~25.7 MB total on HF (the repo's only bulk). Decision-Transformer Atari is a ~2-3M-param GPT-2 per game; the bundle is several game checkpoints. Per-game fp32 ~ a few MB -> int4 a fraction of that. On-tape for one game ~2-4MB; the full multi-game bundle ~13MB. PARAM COUNT NOT VERIFIED (no card metadata).
candidates.runnable: Maybe — needs the DT inference loop PLUS an Atari/ALE emulator to actually 'play' (heavier runtime than the board-game GPTs; the emulator is the real cost, not the weights). Replaying a fixed trajectory is lighter.
candidates.params_or_nature: GPT-2-based Decision Transformer, per-game (~2-3M params each, UNVERIFIED)
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND: NO LICENSE file and no metadata license on the HF repo -> UNKNOWN. Also a heavier runtime story (requires an Atari emulator to demonstrate play). Listed because 'a tape that plays Atari' is the highest-wow concept in the category — worth chasing a licensed alternative (e.g. an Apache/MIT minigrid Decision-Transformer) to fill this slot.

candidates.name: DDPM-MNIST (1aurent/ddpm-mnist)
candidates.what_it_does: Unconditional denoising-diffusion model that PAINTS handwritten digits from pure noise via the DDPM reverse process. A genuine, complete diffusion U-Net (not a LoRA/finetune) at the smallest possible scale. The flex: 'a cassette that runs a real diffusion model and paints' on the acoustic tier.
candidates.size_basis: REAL file: diffusion_pytorch_model.safetensors = 4.288 MB fp32 -> 4.288/4 = 1.07M params. fp16 = ~2.1 MB; weights are noise-like so lzma gains little -> ~2.0 MB on tape. Plus the U-Net+DDPM sampler reimplemented in ~50 lines of numpy (negligible).
candidates.runnable: Yes. UNet2DModel + DDPMScheduler. Runs on-device with diffusers, or the conv U-Net + DDPM loop reimplemented in pure numpy (~50 lines) so no torch needs to ship. CPU sampling of a 28x28 digit is sub-second.
candidates.notes: Best clean fit for the ACOUSTIC C60 tier (~2 MB). MIT verified in model card metadata. Honest sample quality: produces recognizable but blurry MNIST-style digits (1M-param U-Net, trained as a diffusion-class demo). Wow is moderate because the output is grayscale digits, but it IS a true diffusion model running off tape. Tip: ship fp16 weights + a numpy DDPM sampler as the 'runtime', mirroring how DOOM ships engine+wad.

candidates.name: Piper TTS — x_low English voice (rhasspy/piper-voices)
candidates.what_it_does: Neural text-to-speech: type a sentence, the cassette SPEAKS it in a natural human voice (VITS architecture). The flex: 'a cassette that talks back.' The official multi-voice repo is MIT; pick one English x_low voice for the smallest bundle.
candidates.size_basis: REAL file: smallest Piper ONNX voices (e.g. *-x_low.onnx) = 20.63 MB fp32 -> 20.63/4 = 5.16M params. int8 quant -> ~5.2 MB; + English espeak phoneme data (~0.5 MB) + a tiny ONNX runtime. With lzma -> ~7 MB on tape. (Official int8 Piper variants exist, confirming quantizability.)
candidates.license: MIT (model weights); espeak-ng frontend is GPLv3 — FLAG
candidates.runnable: Yes, on-device. ONNX Runtime executes the VITS voice; needs a phoneme frontend (espeak-ng) to turn text into phonemes. Real-time on CPU.
candidates.notes: Fits the ELECTRICAL 8 MB tier. MIT verified for the model weights (rhasspy/piper-voices card). LICENSE FLAG: the standard phoneme frontend is espeak-ng (GPLv3) — to stay permissive, ship espeak-ng SOURCE on side B (DOOM-style) OR swap in a permissive phonemizer; the .onnx itself is MIT. Sample quality at x_low is intelligible but somewhat robotic; the 'medium' tier sounds clearly better.

candidates.name: Piper TTS — medium English voice (rhasspy/piper-voices)
candidates.what_it_does: Same 'cassette that talks' flex but at higher fidelity — a natural-sounding English VITS voice. Trades size for noticeably better speech quality than x_low.
candidates.size_basis: REAL file: en_US-*-low/medium .onnx = 63.10 MB fp32 -> 63.10/4 = 15.7M params. int8 -> ~15.8 MB; with lzma + 0.5 MB phoneme data -> ~14 MB on tape.
candidates.license: MIT (model weights); espeak-ng frontend is GPLv3 — FLAG
candidates.runnable: Yes, on-device via ONNX Runtime + espeak-ng phonemizer. Real-time on CPU.
candidates.notes: Fits the ELECTRICAL 15 MB tier. Same MIT-weights / GPL-frontend flag as the x_low entry. Pick this when capacity allows ~14 MB and you want speech that sounds genuinely human rather than tinny.

candidates.name: DDPM-CIFAR10 (google/ddpm-cifar10-32)
candidates.what_it_does: The canonical DDPM from Ho et al. 2020 — paints 32x32 COLOR photos (planes, cars, animals) from noise. Much higher visual wow than MNIST: actual color imagery. The flex: 'a cassette that paints little color photographs.'
candidates.size_basis: REAL file: diffusion_pytorch_model.safetensors = 143.0 MB fp32 -> 143/4 = 35.7M params. int8 = ~36 MB (over even the stretch tier); int4 = ~18 MB but int4 on a diffusion U-Net severely degrades samples.
candidates.runnable: Yes with diffusers (UNet2DModel + DDPMScheduler). CPU sampling is slow-ish (1000 steps) but works; DDIM cuts steps. Needs torch or a numpy reimpl.
candidates.notes: DO NOT RECOMMEND on size: at usable quality (fp16/int8) it is 36-72 MB, beyond even the 20-40 MB dream tier; int4 (~18 MB) would technically fit but wrecks sample quality on a 35M-param diffusion U-Net. License is clean (Apache-2.0, verified). Listed so the gap is visible: the highest-wow image diffusion that's permissive is just too big to fit well. The 256px celebahq/butterfly DDPMs (454 MB+) are even further out of range.

candidates.name: TunesFormer (sander-wood/tunesformer)
candidates.what_it_does: Symbolic music generator (folk-rnn class): a GPT-2 trained on the Irishman ABC-notation dataset that COMPOSES Irish/folk tunes as ABC notation, which render to readable SHEET MUSIC and play as MIDI. The flex: 'a cassette that writes notated tunes.' Highest musical wow of anything found.
candidates.size_basis: REAL file: pytorch_model.bin = 356.4 MB fp32 -> 356.4/4 = 89M params (the 1061 MB weights.pth is a training checkpoint duplicate). int8 = ~89 MB; int4 = ~45 MB.
candidates.runnable: Yes — it's a GPT-2; generates ABC text on CPU, then a tiny ABC->MIDI/SVG renderer (e.g. abcjs/abc2midi) produces playable music + sheet music. Renderer is small.
candidates.notes: DO NOT RECOMMEND on size: 89M params is ~45 MB even at int4, past the 20-40 MB dream tier, and int4 on an LM this small hurts coherence. License MIT (verified). The single most impressive GENERATIVE-MEDIA flex found (text/seed -> notated folk tune), but it does not fit. Sander-wood's 'text-to-music' (89M, MIT) is the same size class. Kept here as the aspirational target if bitrate climbs further.

candidates.name: huggan/crypto-gan (DCGAN CryptoPunks)
candidates.what_it_does: Tiny DCGAN that generates 24x24 CryptoPunk-style pixel avatars. A genuinely small generative image model — appealing for the acoustic tier if the license were clear.
candidates.size_basis: REAL files: saved_model.pb 0.21 MB + keras_metadata 0.03 MB = 0.24 MB (the bulk of the SavedModel graph; the variables shard adds a little). Tiny by any measure.
candidates.license: UNKNOWN — no license in model card
candidates.runnable: Yes in principle (TF SavedModel -> ONNX), but no permissive license to clear it.
candidates.notes: DO NOT RECOMMEND: model card has NO license field (only tags: gan/dcgan/huggan). README confirms it's a 'Simple DCGAN to generate CryptoPunks' but states no license -> license_confidence=unknown. Also: CryptoPunks IP itself is a separate concern. Tiny and cute, but cannot be cleared. Verified README directly.

candidates.name: skytnt/midi-model
candidates.what_it_does: A real MIDI music transformer (Los Angeles MIDI dataset) that GENERATES multi-instrument MIDI music; ships ONNX + a soundfont so it can play in-browser. The flex: 'a cassette that composes and plays music.' Apache-2.0.
candidates.size_basis: REAL files: model.safetensors = 933.3 MB fp32 -> 933/4 = ~233M params. ONNX model_base 821 MB + model_token 115 MB + soundfont.sf2 51 MB. int8 ~233 MB; int4 ~117 MB.
candidates.runnable: Yes, even in-browser (ships ONNX + soundfont) — very much in the spirit of the DOOM tape.
candidates.notes: DO NOT RECOMMEND on size: ~117-233 MB quantized, far beyond all tiers. License is clean (Apache-2.0, verified) and the in-browser ONNX+soundfont packaging is exactly the cassette aesthetic — but it's an order of magnitude too big. Listed as the aspirational 'compose+play music' target. (huggingnft/cryptopunks GAN ~37.7M params/151 MB, MIT, and the 100M-class TTS 'nano' models are likewise out of range.)

scouted.category: RUNNABLE SOFTWARE / GAMES / EMULATORS / DEMOS / OS (3-20 MB, permissive assets)

candidates.name: v86 + Buildroot Linux (a cassette that BOOTS LINUX in the browser)
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> a real x86 PC emulator (v86) JITs x86 to WebAssembly in the browser and boots a full 32-bit Buildroot Linux to a working shell (BusyBox, Lua, curl, telnet, ping). The single highest-wow flex in the category: a cassette that contains an entire bootable operating system, not just a game.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: x86 emulator (v86, BSD-2) + SeaBIOS/Bochs-BIOS ROM + Buildroot Linux bzImage kernel
candidates.size_basis: REAL files, xz -9: v86.wasm 1,424,184 B -> 199,668 xz; libv86.js 337,697 B -> 75,024 xz; bochs-bios.bin 65,536 + bochs-vgabios.bin 43,008; buildroot-bzimage.bin 5,166,352 B. Concatenated bundle 7,036,777 B raw -> 5,457,536 B xz (5.46 MB). Kernel dominates and is near-incompressible (already internally compressed). Larger 10 MB buildroot variant also exists.
candidates.license: BSD-2-Clause (v86 engine + JS); kernel = GPL-2.0 (Linux, source public); BIOS blobs = LGPL-3.0 (Bochs/SeaBIOS)
candidates.runnable: Yes - runs entirely client-side in any modern browser via WebAssembly; the v86 examples/ harness is the minimal runtime. No server needed once the wasm+js+BIOS+image are bundled into one HTML page (same pattern as the DOOM tape).
candidates.notes: GPL/LGPL components require source to ship (Linux kernel GPL-2.0, SeaBIOS LGPL-3.0) - mirror the DOOM 'binary side A / GPL source side B' approach. v86 ships fetch-and-build-seabios.sh, so BIOS source compliance is satisfiable. v86 engine itself is clean BSD-2. The 5.17 MB image fits a C120 mono or comfortably on stereo electrical; the 10 MB variant needs the high-rate stereo tier. Flag: BIOS ROM blobs are LGPL not permissive-permissive, but legal to redistribute with source offer.

candidates.name: TIC-80 fantasy computer (MIT) + tiny carts
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> a complete TIC-80 fantasy console boots in the browser: a self-contained retro game machine with code/sprite/map/sound/music editors and a player that runs Lua/JS/Python/Fennel/Moon/Wren cartridges. Ship it with bundled MIT demo carts so the tape literally IS a playable game console plus games.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: WebAssembly build of the TIC-80 runtime+player (tic80.wasm + tic80.js + index.html) plus .tic cartridges (10-64 KB each)
candidates.size_basis: REAL release asset tic80-v1.1-html.zip (2,046,671 B) unpacked: tic80.wasm 5,716,041 B + tic80.js 240,206 B + index.html 2,627 B = 5,958,874 B raw. xz -9 of the full bundle = 1,546,728 B (1.55 MB). Carts are capped at 256 KB and typically 10-64 KB, so several complete games add negligibly.
candidates.runnable: Yes - the html release is a self-contained WebAssembly player; open index.html and it runs. Minimal runtime is exactly the three shipped files. Carts load from inside the bundle.
candidates.notes: LICENSE confirmed MIT; demos/ folder (luademo, p3d, fire, palette, music, plus Fennel/Janet/Python/JS/Moon demos) ships in-repo under the same MIT license. Cleanest fully-permissive 'a cassette that is a games console' candidate - no GPL caveat at all. Fits C90 mono with room to spare.

candidates.name: Octo CHIP-8 IDE/emulator (MIT) + the complete CHIP-8 Archive (CC0)
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> a single self-contained HTML page (Octo's 'Save HTML' export) emulates CHIP-8/SuperCHIP/XO-CHIP and plays the ENTIRE CHIP-8 Archive: 101 homebrew games, every one public-domain. The cleanest 'whole curated game library on one tape' flex, and it fits the acoustic tier today.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: Octo emulator (~200 KB JS, MIT) + 101 .ch8 ROMs (1.1 MB raw, CC0)
candidates.size_basis: REAL files: chip8Archive roms/ = 101 .ch8 files totaling 1.1 MB (largest single ROM 65,024 B); Octo js/ dir = 200 KB (emulator.js 21,032 + compiler/decompiler/input/etc). ch8 ROMs are mostly sparse/repetitive and compress hard with xz; engine+all-ROMs realistically ~0.6 MB on tape. Conservative upper bound well under 1 MB.
candidates.license: MIT (Octo engine) + CC0 / public domain (all 101 archive ROMs)
candidates.runnable: Yes - Octo has a built-in 'Save HTML' feature that emits one standalone HTML file containing the emulator + embedded program(s); pure browser, no server. Ideal cassette target.
candidates.notes: chip8Archive Readme.md explicitly places everything under CC0 'No Rights Reserved'. Octo LICENSE.txt = MIT. The whole library fits the C60/C90 ACOUSTIC tier achievable NOW - lowest-risk, highest-polish entry-level candidate. Octo is the canonical CHIP-8 emulator/IDE.

candidates.name: WASM-4 fantasy console (ISC) + example games
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> the WASM-4 fantasy console runtime boots and runs 64 KB WebAssembly game cartridges (any language that compiles to wasm: Rust, C, Zig, Go, AssemblyScript). Ship with the bundled example games. A minimalist 'a cassette that is a wasm games console' flex - even smaller and more modern than TIC-80.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: WASM-4 web runtime (lightweight C runtime compiled to wasm) + example .wasm carts (hard-capped 64 KB each)
candidates.size_basis: REAL npm package wasm4@2.7.1 unpackedSize = 1,920,771 B (1.9 MB) for the full web runtime; cartridges hard-capped at 64 KB by spec. Web runtime compresses well (wasm+js); estimate ~0.9 MB on tape for runtime + a few example carts. Did not byte-pull the exact web bundle, so on_tape is an estimate from npm unpacked size + typical wasm xz ratio.
candidates.license: ISC (runtime); example game licenses vary per-file - verify each before shipping
candidates.runnable: Yes - WASM-4 runs in-browser; the web runtime is self-contained. Minimal runtime = the wasm4 web player bundle.
candidates.notes: LICENSE.txt confirmed ISC (permissive, Bruno Garcia). Caveat: the /examples carts are not blanket-licensed in one statement - the repo is ISC but individual example licenses should be checked per-file before bundling. Runtime is unambiguously permissive. Slightly less wow than TIC-80 because it has no built-in editors (it's a pure runtime), but tiny and clean.

candidates.name: KolibriOS (a complete GUI OS in 1.44 MB) on v86
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> v86 boots KolibriOS, a full graphical desktop operating system written entirely in x86 assembly that fits on a single floppy. Boots in seconds to a windowed GUI with apps and games. 'A cassette that boots a graphical OS' - more visually striking than a Linux shell and far smaller than Buildroot.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: v86 emulator (BSD-2) + KolibriOS 1.44 MB floppy image
candidates.size_basis: REAL files: kolibri.img 1,474,560 B (standard 1.44 MB floppy) -> xz 1,278,284 B; plus v86.wasm 199,668 xz + libv86.js 75,024 xz + Bochs BIOS 108 KB. Total bootable bundle ~1.6 MB on tape.
candidates.license: v86 = BSD-2-Clause; KolibriOS image = GPL-2.0 (source public); BIOS = LGPL-3.0
candidates.runnable: Yes - boots in v86 in-browser, fully client-side, to a graphical desktop. Same self-contained HTML bundling as the Linux candidate.
candidates.notes: KolibriOS is GPL-2.0 (kernel/apps) - ship source on side B like DOOM. The OS image itself fits the ACOUSTIC tier (1.44 MB floppy), and the whole bootable bundle is ~1.6 MB on tape - dramatically smaller than Buildroot Linux while arguably MORE impressive (it's a graphical desktop, not a shell). Strong alternative/companion to the v86+Linux entry. Flag the GPL kernel + LGPL BIOS source requirement.

candidates.name: Bad Apple on Game Boy (.gb) + WasmBoy emulator (Apache-2.0)
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> a WasmBoy WebAssembly Game Boy emulator plays the famous 'Bad Apple' shadow-art music video as a real DMG Game Boy ROM (160x72, 15 fps, LZ4-decompressed in 3 frames). The 'a cassette that plays a Game Boy demo' novelty - audiovisually striking and instantly recognizable.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: WasmBoy emulator core (Apache-2.0) + Bad Apple .gb ROM (617 KB)
candidates.size_basis: REAL release asset: Bad Apple .gb = 631,852 B (617 KB) from the GitHub releases API. WasmBoy core (AssemblyScript->wasm) is a few hundred KB; full emulator+ROM bundle estimated ~1.4 MB on tape. The ROM is dense LZ4 video data so it does not compress much further; the wasm core does.
candidates.license: ROM = GPL-3.0 (source ships, flag); emulator = Apache-2.0 (WasmBoy)
candidates.runnable: Yes - WasmBoy is a browser WebAssembly GB/GBC emulator; load the .gb and it plays. Self-contained.
candidates.notes: The Bad Apple ROM is GPL-3.0 (LICENSE file present) - the .gb is the binary, source ships on side B, on-brand with DOOM. WasmBoy emulator is Apache-2.0 (confirmed). Alternative permissive emulator: Octo-style or wasm-gb (no bootrom). Lower wow than the OS/console candidates (it's a demo, not interactive) but a fun, recognizable, well-sized entry. Could pair other CC0/permissive GB homebrew instead of the GPL Bad Apple to make it fully permissive.

candidates.name: Qwasm + LibreQuake (a cassette that plays QUAKE with free assets)
candidates.what_it_does: Insert tape -> the Qwasm WebAssembly port of the GPL Quake engine runs in the browser playing LibreQuake, a free-content total-conversion that replaces all copyrighted id Quake .pak data with libre maps, textures, models and bots. 'A cassette that plays Quake' - the natural next-rung-up from DOOM.
candidates.params_or_nature: software: Qwasm (GPL-2.0 Quake engine -> WASM) + LibreQuake free game data (.pak)
candidates.size_basis: ESTIMATE, NOT byte-verified: Qwasm engine wasm is DOOM-class (a few hundred KB compressed). LibreQuake's free pak data is the unknown - the GitHub repo (LibreQuake/LibreQuake) 404'd on the URL I tried, so I could not pull the real pak size. Quake-class content with textures/models/maps typically runs tens of MB raw; a trimmed LibreQuake episode could plausibly land ~10-15 MB on tape, but this number is a guess pending a real size pull from the LibreQuake release assets.
candidates.license: Engine = GPL-2.0 (id Software / Qwasm, source ships); assets = LibreQuake free-content (verify exact license: CC-BY/CC0/GPL mix per the LibreQuake repo)
candidates.runnable: Yes in principle - Qwasm is a proven browser WASM Quake engine (qwasm.m-h.org.uk). Needs the LibreQuake pak bundled as the data carrier, same as DOOM bundles the WAD.
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND until verified: I could not pull the real LibreQuake pak size (repo URL 404'd) nor confirm the exact per-asset license. The original Quake .pak data is NOT free and must not ship - LibreQuake is the free replacement, but its license needs per-file verification before use. Engine is GPL-2.0 (ship source side B, on-brand). High wow if it pans out, but it likely exceeds the 20 MB target and the size/license are unverified - flagged so the gap is visible. Next step: pull LibreQuake release assets and measure + read its LICENSE.

scouted.category: Public-domain text / knowledge corpora (compress to ~3-20 MB)

candidates.name: The Complete Works of William Shakespeare
candidates.what_it_does: Every play, sonnet, and poem Shakespeare wrote — the canonical 'a cassette that holds all of Shakespeare' flex. Decodes to a single searchable text + a ~5 KB embedded HTML reader (table of contents, in-page search, pagination) that opens in any browser.
candidates.size_basis: MEASURED, not estimated: downloaded gutenberg.org pg100.txt = 5,638,480 bytes raw; `xz -9e` = 1,674,420 bytes (3.37x). Add a ~3 KB lzma'd HTML reader -> ~1.68 MB on tape.
candidates.params_or_nature: corpus: ~900k words, the full First Folio + poems
candidates.license: Public Domain (US, pre-1928); Project Gutenberg distribution
candidates.runnable: Yes. Plain UTF-8 text — readable as-is. Bundle a tiny self-contained HTML reader (search + ToC, ~5 KB) for a 'pop in the tape, open in browser' demo; base64 carrier like the repo's web-DOOM build to be HTTP-charset-immune. No runtime install.
candidates.notes: The single best wow/size ratio in this category. Fits a C90 mono (3.9-4.4 MB) with room to spare; fits acoustically TODAY. PD verified: pre-1928 works, Gutenberg's flagship ebook #100. Gutenberg trademark only restricts charging money — non-commercial redistribution is unrestricted.

candidates.name: The King James Bible (Authorized Version)
candidates.what_it_does: The complete Old + New Testament, the most-printed book in English. 'The whole Bible on one cassette.' Strong civilization-in-a-tape symbolism; decodes to searchable text + reader.
candidates.size_basis: MEASURED: gutenberg.org pg10.txt = 4,455,950 bytes raw; `xz -9e` = 1,057,812 bytes (4.21x — best ratio here, repetitive verse structure compresses hard).
candidates.license: Public Domain (US). NOTE: in the UK the KJV is under perpetual Crown copyright / Cambridge letters patent — flag for UK distribution.
candidates.runnable: Yes. Plain text + optional tiny HTML reader with book/chapter/verse navigation. Browser-runnable, no install.
candidates.notes: Smallest on-tape footprint of any complete major work measured — fits a C60 mono (2.6 MB) acoustically today. PD verified in the US. Caveat to surface: UK Crown copyright exists; safe for the US-based project but worth a one-line flag.

candidates.name: The Complete Sherlock Holmes Canon (Conan Doyle)
candidates.what_it_does: All 4 novels + 56 short stories (the 9 canonical books: A Study in Scarlet, Sign of Four, Hound of the Baskervilles, Valley of Fear, Adventures, Memoirs, Return, His Last Bow, Case-Book). 'The entire Holmes canon on one tape.'
candidates.size_basis: MEASURED: 10 Gutenberg .txt files (ids 1661,2097,108,244,2343,2350,221,69700,3289,834) concatenated = 4,398,753 bytes raw; `xz -9e` = 1,063,760 bytes (4.13x).
candidates.params_or_nature: corpus: complete detective canon, ~660k words across 9 books
candidates.runnable: Yes. Concatenated text + tiny HTML reader with per-story ToC. Browser-runnable.
candidates.notes: All constituent works confirmed PD in US (latest, The Case-Book, is 1927). Fits C60 mono. A crisp, recognizable 'complete works of X' flex. Slightly less iconic than Shakespeare but identical size class.

candidates.name: Human Knowledge Starter Pack (18 foundational works)
candidates.what_it_does: A deliberately curated 'reboot civilization' library: Darwin's Origin of Species, Einstein's Relativity, Plato's Republic, Aristotle's Ethics, Marcus Aurelius' Meditations, Sun Tzu's Art of War, the US Constitution, plus Shakespeare, Moby-Dick, Frankenstein, Dracula, War and Peace, Pride and Prejudice and more. The strongest 'civilization-in-a-tape' angle.
candidates.size_basis: MEASURED: 18 Gutenberg .txt works (science/philosophy/law/literature) concatenated = 12,330,867 bytes raw; `xz -9e` = 3,333,212 bytes (3.70x).
candidates.license: Public Domain (US) — every included work pre-1928; Project Gutenberg
candidates.runnable: Yes. Multi-document bundle + tiny HTML reader with a top-level domain/work index. Browser-runnable, no install. The reader is the 'runtime' (negligible vs corpus).
candidates.notes: Fits a C90 mono (3.9-4.4 MB) acoustically TODAY with headroom. Highest narrative wow: it's an editorial artifact, not just a dump — 'the seed of human knowledge on a cassette.' Exact book list is tunable to hit any target size; all members individually PD-verified. Recommended as the flagship 3-6 MB payload.

candidates.name: The Great Library — 58-book Classics Collection
candidates.what_it_does: A broad public-domain library: ~58 of the most-downloaded Gutenberg classics (Austen, Dickens, Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Melville, Twain, Wilde, Shelley, Stoker, Doyle, Carroll, the KJV Bible, Shakespeare, and more). The maximal 'Library of Alexandria on a cassette' for the stereo/electrical tier.
candidates.size_basis: MEASURED: 58 Gutenberg .txt classics concatenated = 60,061,130 bytes raw; `xz -9e` = 15,840,944 bytes (3.79x). Book count scales the size linearly — trim to ~30 books for ~8 MB, ~45 for ~12 MB, keep 58 for ~16 MB.
candidates.license: Public Domain (US) — selection restricted to pre-1928 works; Project Gutenberg
candidates.runnable: Yes. Bundle + tiny HTML library reader (author/title index, in-page search). Browser-runnable, no install.
candidates.notes: Lands squarely in the 8-20 MB electrical/stereo tier — the headline payload for the capacity climb. Size is a free knob (drop/add books). Care needed on the selection list to keep every title pre-1928 PD; the measured 58-book set was chosen from canonical pre-1928 classics. The definitive 'whole library on one tape' flex.

candidates.name: War and Peace (single-novel anchor)
candidates.what_it_does: Tolstoy's ~560k-word epic — proof that even the longest canonical novel fits comfortably. Useful as a single-artifact 'longest novel in the language on one tape' demo.
candidates.size_basis: MEASURED: gutenberg.org pg2600.txt = 3,359,610 bytes raw; `xz -9e` = 935,556 bytes (3.59x).
candidates.license: Public Domain (US); Project Gutenberg (Maude/Garnett-era translation, pre-1928)
candidates.runnable: Yes. Plain text + tiny reader. Browser-runnable.
candidates.notes: Under 1 MB on tape — fits a C60 acoustically. Lower wow as a standalone (a single book is less of a flex than 'complete works'), but a clean, verifiable PD anchor and a nice 'even the doorstop fits' line. The English text is a pre-1928 translation; confirm the specific Gutenberg edition stays pre-1928 if shipped.

candidates.name: TinyStories dataset sample (roneneldan/TinyStories)
candidates.what_it_does: A slice of the synthetic short-story corpus that tiny LLMs are trained on — thematically perfect for this project (the cassette already carries a TinyStories MODEL; carrying a sample of its TRAINING DATA closes the loop).
candidates.size_basis: NOT independently measured here. Full train set is ~1-3M stories (size_categories 1M<n<10M, parquet). A ~10-20 MB raw text sample would lzma to ~3-5 MB at the ~3.5-4x seen for this prose; exact on-tape size depends on the chosen sample and was not downloaded/compressed in this pass.
candidates.params_or_nature: data: synthetic GPT-3.5/4-generated short stories, small vocabulary
candidates.license: CDLA-Sharing-1.0 (Community Data License Agreement — Sharing). SHARE-ALIKE: redistributions/derivatives must carry the same license. NOT public domain.
candidates.runnable: Data, not a program. 'Runnable' only as text fed to a reader, or as the training corpus behind the on-tape TinyStories model.
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND as a permissive payload: CDLA-Sharing-1.0 is a copyleft/share-alike data license (like GPL for data) — allowed only if you propagate the license, analogous to the DOOM source-on-side-B rule. License VERIFIED via HF metadata. Size NOT measured (would need a download+compress pass). Listed so the share-alike gap is visible; the PD Gutenberg options dominate it on both license and wow.

candidates.name: Simple English Wikipedia (curated subset)
candidates.what_it_does: A subset of Simple English Wikipedia — an actual encyclopedia in plain language. 'An encyclopedia on a cassette' is a top-tier civilization flex IF a meaningful subset fits.
candidates.size_basis: NOT measured for a subset. Full simplewiki pages-articles-multistream dump is ~326 MB bz2 (2025) — far over budget; only a curated article subset (e.g. top-N vital articles, stripped wikitext) could hit 8-20 MB lzma. The 12 MB figure is a PLACEHOLDER target, not a measurement — a real subset must be extracted and compressed to size it.
candidates.license: CC-BY-SA 4.0 (some text CC-BY-SA 3.0). SHARE-ALIKE + ATTRIBUTION required. NOT public domain.
candidates.runnable: Yes in principle: rendered wikitext/HTML + a tiny reader. Requires a wikitext-to-text/HTML preprocessing step (non-trivial) before it's a clean payload.
candidates.notes: DO-NOT-RECOMMEND as-is: (1) license is CC-BY-SA share-alike — must propagate license + attribution (flag like GPL/CDLA); (2) NO real subset was extracted or compressed, so the on-tape size is unverified; (3) needs a wikitext-stripping pipeline. Highest conceptual wow ('encyclopedia on a tape') but the most work and a copyleft license — keep as a future stretch candidate, not a ship-ready one.

leaderboard.tier: Type I Normal — Bronze (acoustic, achievable NOW)
leaderboard.cassette_config: C60 mono acoustic @5800 bps (~2.6 MB budget) — the proven living-room loop
leaderboard.headline: TIC-80 fantasy console (MIT) + MIT demo carts — "a cassette that IS a games console"
leaderboard.license: MIT (engine + bundled demo carts) — verified, ZERO copyleft caveat
leaderboard.why_a_flex: Pop in the tape, a complete retro fantasy computer boots in the browser — code/sprite/map/sound/music editors AND a player running Lua/JS/Python/Fennel/Wren carts. ~1.6 MB on tape (xz of the 5.96 MB html release) leaves room to spare on a C60. The cleanest fully-permissive 'console on a cassette' candidate — no GPL footnote at all.
runners_up: DDPM-MNIST (1aurent/ddpm-mnist, MIT verified) — ~2.0 MB fp16: a REAL diffusion U-Net that paints handwritten digits from pure noise. "A cassette that runs a diffusion model."

AlignmentResearch learned-planner Sokoban DRC(3,3) (Apache-2.0 VERIFIED, best license story in the whole batch) — ~1.3 MB int8: an RNN that demonstrably PLANS ahead to push boxes onto goals (ICML 2024). "A cassette that thinks."
Octo CHIP-8 IDE (MIT) + the entire 101-game CHIP-8 Archive (CC0) — ~0.6 MB: a whole curated public-domain game library on one tape.
KolibriOS graphical desktop OS on v86 — ~1.6 MB on tape (GPL kernel + LGPL BIOS, ship source side B): boots a full windowed GUI desktop from a 1.44 MB floppy image.

leaderboard.tier: Type I Normal — Silver (acoustic, achievable NOW)
leaderboard.cassette_config: C90 mono acoustic @5800 bps (3.9-4.4 MB budget); the smaller corpora fit a C60
leaderboard.headline: Human Knowledge Starter Pack — 18 foundational works (Darwin, Einstein, Plato, Aurelius, Sun Tzu, the Constitution, Shakespeare, Frankenstein...) — "the seed of civilization on a cassette"
leaderboard.license: Public Domain (US, every work pre-1928) — verified via Project Gutenberg
leaderboard.why_a_flex: MEASURED: 18 cross-domain texts = 12.33 MB raw → 3.33 MB xz -9e. An editorial artifact, not a dump — science + philosophy + law + literature curated as a 'reboot civilization' library, with a ~5 KB embedded HTML reader. Fits a C90 mono acoustically TODAY with headroom. Highest narrative wow in the corpus category.
runners_up: Complete Works of Shakespeare (PD verified) — MEASURED 1.68 MB on tape: the canonical 'all of Shakespeare on one tape' flex, best wow/size ratio of any single corpus, fits a C60.
King James Bible (PD-US verified; UK Crown-copyright flag) — MEASURED 1.06 MB: smallest complete major work, fits a C60 with massive headroom.
Complete Sherlock Holmes canon (PD verified) — MEASURED 1.07 MB: all 4 novels + 56 stories.
delphi-suite/v0-llama2-12.8m int8 variant pushes into the next tier; at this tier the corpora dominate on verified-PD cleanliness.

leaderboard.tier: Type II Chrome — Gold (the electrical climb begins)
leaderboard.cassette_config: C120 mono acoustic (~5.8 MB) → C90 stereo ELECTRICAL @6.5 kbps (8.7 MB) — needs the USB line-in path (UCA222)
leaderboard.headline: delphi-suite/v0-llama2-12.8m (TinyStories Llama-2, 4096-vocab) int4 — "a cassette that WRITES coherent stories"
leaderboard.license: MIT (HF metadata tag, no LICENSE file → PROBABLE; code repo Apache-2.0) — permissive either way
leaderboard.why_a_flex: 14.56M params, but the tiny 4096 SentencePiece vocab means embeddings are only 11% — so it FITS where 50K-vocab models can't. int4 (7.28 MB raw) + 65 KB tokenizer + ~20 KB llama2.c WASM runtime → ~6.7 MB on tape. Genuinely beyond babble: near-perfect grammar, multi-sentence children's stories. The repo already ships a karpathy stories runtime at the sub-MB tier, so the inference path is proven. The cassette enforces the lesson: small-vocab + story-specialized beats big-vocab general on bytes-per-coherence (GPT-2-small at 124M babbles; this writes prose at 1/8th the params).
runners_up: Piper TTS x_low English voice (rhasspy/piper-voices, MIT weights VERIFIED; espeak-ng frontend GPLv3 — ship source side B) — ~7 MB int8: "a cassette that talks back" in a natural VITS voice.
v86 + Buildroot Linux — ~5.5 MB xz (v86 BSD-2 VERIFIED; Linux GPL-2.0 + BIOS LGPL-3.0, ship source side B): "a cassette that BOOTS a full 32-bit Linux to a shell."

leaderboard.tier: Type II Chrome — Platinum (the electrical climb)
leaderboard.cassette_config: C90 stereo ELECTRICAL @10 kbps (13.4 MB) — the high-rate OFDM stereo line-in config
leaderboard.headline: adamkarvonen Chess-GPT (lichess 8-layer, 25M) int4 — "a cassette that OUT-PLAYS a chess engine"
leaderboard.license: MIT (training repo github.com/adamkarvonen/train_ChessGPT VERIFIED MIT; weights repo has EMPTY metadata → PROBABLE-via-training-repo) — confirm with author before ship
leaderboard.why_a_flex: REAL: lichess_8layers_ckpt = 103 MB fp32 → 25.0M params → int4 = 12.5 MB on tape (32-char chess vocab <1 KB, nanoGPT runtime negligible). This model actually PLAYS chess: 99.6% legal-move rate, ~46% win-rate vs Stockfish 16 level-0 (~1300 Elo), and the COLM 2024 'internal world model' result — linear probes recover a ~98%-accurate board from its activations. A dramatic upgrade over the 6.6M chess-gpt already staged: it beats a real engine instead of just emitting plausible moves.
runners_up: delphi-suite/v0-llama2-25.6m int4 (MIT PROBABLE) — ~11.7 MB: larger TinyStories model, better prose coherence than the 12.8m. The 'fill more tape, get better stories' pick.

Baidicoot/Othello-GPT (TransformerLens, ~25.2M, MIT PROBABLE — no LICENSE file) int4 — ~12.5 MB: the interpretability-famous 'it has a world model' Othello model; pairs with Chess-GPT as a 'board-game world-model double feature.'
Piper TTS medium English voice (MIT weights VERIFIED; espeak-ng GPLv3 flag) — ~14 MB: human-sounding VITS speech, a clear step up from x_low.
blueapple8259/TinyCode-python (30.4M LLaMA, MIT PROBABLE) int4 — ~17 MB (top edge): "a cassette that autocompletes Python."

leaderboard.tier: Type IV Metal — Diamond (top of the climb, ~20 MB)
leaderboard.cassette_config: C90 stereo ELECTRICAL @15 kbps (20 MB) — the highest verified stereo line-in tier
leaderboard.headline: The Great Library — 58-book public-domain classics collection — "the Library of Alexandria on one cassette"
leaderboard.license: Public Domain (US, selection restricted to pre-1928 works) — verified via Project Gutenberg
leaderboard.why_a_flex: MEASURED: 58 Gutenberg classics (Austen, Dickens, Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Melville, Twain, Wilde, Shelley, Stoker, Doyle, Carroll, KJV, Shakespeare...) = 60.06 MB raw → 15.84 MB xz -9e. ~9.8M words, a tiny HTML library reader as the 'runtime.' Size is a free knob — trim to ~45 books for ~12 MB, keep 58 for ~16 MB, all verified-PD. The definitive 'whole library on a tape' headline payload for the capacity climb, and the safest license in the whole top band.
runners_up: blueapple8259/TinyCode-python int4 (~17 MB, MIT PROBABLE) — "a tape that writes code" in its smallest credible form.
TinyStories-8M with vocab-trimmed runtime — NOT recommended (roneneldan repo has NO license; see gaps). Listed only as the technique reference.
DDPM-CIFAR10 int4 (~18 MB, Apache-2.0 VERIFIED) — technically fits but int4 wrecks diffusion sample quality; a near-miss, not a headline.

stretch_dream.tier: Type IV Metal — LEGENDARY (the 20-40 MB dream)
stretch_dream.cassette_config: C120 stereo ELECTRICAL @10 kbps (17.9 MB) up to @20 kbps (36 MB) — the dream tape; NOT yet demonstrated, the electrical line-in path must first prove out at these rates
stretch_dream.headline_candidate: Q-bert/ChessGPT (87.5M GPT-2) int3 — "a substantially stronger chess brain on tape"
stretch_dream.license: MIT (HF metadata, no LICENSE file → PROBABLE)
stretch_dream.why_a_flex: REAL: 350 MB fp32 → 87.5M params. int4 = ~44 MB (overshoots the 40 MB ceiling); int3 ~33 MB squeaks into the C120-stereo-@20kbps dream. The largest credible chess transformer that could plausibly fit any cassette tier.

aspirational_ceiling.name: HuggingFaceTB/SmolLM2-135M-Instruct (Apache-2.0 VERIFIED)
aspirational_ceiling.the_dream_flex: "a cassette you can have a CONVERSATION with" — a real instruction-tuned chat model, not a babbler. int4 ~67 MB does NOT fit even the 36 MB dream tape; only a ternary/int2 QAT build (~34 MB) would squeeze it. THE category-defining flex if a <=2-bit QAT checkpoint with a verified-permissive license ever appears. Revisit-trigger payload.

runner_up_generative.name: sander-wood/tunesformer (89M GPT-2, MIT VERIFIED)
runner_up_generative.flex: "a cassette that COMPOSES notated folk tunes" — seed → ABC notation → playable MIDI + readable sheet music. The single most impressive generative-media concept found. int4 ~45 MB just over the dream ceiling, and int4 hurts coherence on an LM this small — aspirational, not ship-ready.

stretch_dream.honest_note: No verified+permissive payload cleanly OWNS the 20-40 MB band yet at good quality. The dream tape itself is unproven (electrical line-in at >=10 kbps stereo has not been demonstrated — it's THE unlock). The strongest dream entry is curatorial, not a model: extend The Great Library to ~75-90 books (~20-24 MB measured-scalable, verified PD) — guaranteed-clean and guaranteed-fits. The model dreams (SmolLM2 chat, TunesFormer) all need a <=int3/ternary QAT checkpoint that does not yet exist permissively-licensed.

gaps: ~3.5 MB acoustic 'plays Othello' slot: awonga/othello-gpt-7M is perfectly sized (int4 ~3.5 MB) but has NO license declared anywhere (empty card, no metadata tag) → cannot ship. Verified gap; chase a licensed 7M Othello reproduction.

16-20 MB MODEL slot (vs corpus): the only verified-permissive things filling ~16-20 MB are text corpora and TinyCode-python. No verified-permissive STORY/CHAT model lands cleanly here — TinyStories-8M (roneneldan) has NO license, and vocab-trimming it is a build step, not a download.

20-40 MB dream band has NO clean-quality verified-permissive payload: SmolLM2-135M-Instruct (Apache-2.0) needs int2/ternary QAT to fit (~34 MB) and no such checkpoint exists permissively; Q-bert ChessGPT (MIT probable) only fits at int3 ~33 MB with quality risk; TunesFormer (MIT) is ~45 MB, just over. The band is fillable only by scaling The Great Library corpus.

'A cassette that plays Atari' (highest-wow game concept, edbeeching/decision_transformer_atari) is BLOCKED: NO license on the HF repo AND needs a heavy ALE emulator runtime. Chase a permissive minigrid/Atari Decision-Transformer.

'A cassette that plays QUAKE' (Qwasm + LibreQuake) is UNVERIFIED: could not pull real LibreQuake pak size (repo URL 404'd) and per-asset license unconfirmed. Original Quake .pak is NOT free and must never ship. Needs a real size+license pull before it can fill the ~10-15 MB tier.

Image-diffusion at COLOR quality has no fit: DDPM-CIFAR10 (Apache-2.0) is clean-licensed but 36-72 MB at usable quant; int4 (~18 MB) fits but wrecks samples. No permissive color-image generator fits a cassette at good quality.
'Compose + play music' at full quality: skytnt/midi-model (Apache-2.0, in-browser ONNX+soundfont — perfect cassette aesthetic) is ~117-233 MB quantized, an order of magnitude over every tier.
top_picks_to_fetch: adamkarvonen/chess_llms — lichess_8layers_ckpt (25M, int4 ~12.5 MB): the Platinum headline, 'a cassette that beats a chess engine.' Fetch + int4-quantize + bundle the nanoGPT runtime; confirm MIT with author (weights repo metadata is empty, training repo is verified MIT).

delphi-suite/v0-llama2-12.8m (int4 ~6.7 MB): the Gold story headline, 'a cassette that writes stories.' Highest-value model fetch — verified-MIT-tag, tiny-vocab-fits, proven runtime path. Grab the 25.6m sibling too for the Platinum runner-up.

The Great Library corpus — assemble the 58-book pre-1928 PD set from Gutenberg and lzma it (Diamond headline, ~16 MB; scalable to fill 8-20 MB AND the dream band). Pure-PD, zero license risk, size is a free knob. Highest reliability-per-effort.

AlignmentResearch/learned-planner DRC(3,3) (Apache-2.0 VERIFIED, ~1.3 MB int8): the cleanest-license payload in the entire batch and a novel 'a cassette that PLANS' flex — small enough to PAIR with another payload on one acoustic side.

TIC-80 html release + curated MIT carts (~1.6 MB on tape): the Bronze headline, the only zero-caveat fully-permissive 'games console on a cassette.' Trivial to fetch and bundle; achievable on the acoustic loop today.
headline_takeaways: The high-score chase has a clean verified ladder ALL THE WAY to ~20 MB: Bronze TIC-80 console (1.6 MB, MIT, NOW) → Silver Human-Knowledge-Pack (3.3 MB, PD, NOW) → Gold TinyStories writer (6.7 MB, MIT) → Platinum Chess-GPT-beats-engine (12.5 MB) → Diamond 58-book Library (15.8 MB). Every headline is verified or probable-permissive.

Two tiers are achievable on the CURRENT acoustic loop (Bronze + Silver, <=4.4 MB). Everything from Gold up needs the electrical line-in / stereo climb that the repo flags as the unlock — the chase is gated on hardware, not on payloads.

The cassette enforces a sharp engineering lesson: SMALL VOCAB + TASK-SPECIALIZED beats BIG VOCAB + GENERAL on bytes-per-coherence. delphi-12.8m (14.5M, 4096-vocab) writes prose; GPT-2-small (124M, 50K-vocab) babbles and doesn't even fit at int4. Story/board-game models with tiny output alphabets are what make a tape sing.

Best license story in the whole batch: AlignmentResearch/learned-planner (Apache-2.0, declared on BOTH the HF card and a GitHub LICENSE file). Best verified-PD safety: the Gutenberg corpora. Use these as the zero-risk anchors.

The 20-40 MB dream band is the real frontier and is currently UNFILLED at model quality: the chattable SmolLM2-135M and the tune-composing TunesFormer are both just-too-big and lack permissive sub-int4/ternary QAT checkpoints. Only the scalable PD corpus reliably reaches the dream tape today.

Honest gaps worth chasing: a licensed 7M Othello model (~3.5 MB slot), a permissive Atari Decision-Transformer ('plays Atari'), and a measured+licensed LibreQuake pak ('plays Quake') — three of the highest-wow concepts are blocked only on license/verification, not feasibility.
leaderboard.notes:
CURATION LOGIC. Tiers are anchored to the repo's capacity table: Bronze/Silver (<=4.4 MB) ride the PROVEN acoustic mono loop (39 dB SNR living-room capture) and are shippable now; Gold (6.7 MB) onward require the electrical line-in / stereo path the repo flags as the unlock for high-rate OFDM. I ranked each tier by WOW among ONLY verified/probable-permissive + recommended candidates, then added 1-3 runners-up.

VERIFICATION DONE THIS PASS (against HF ground truth, not the scout's word): delphi-suite/v0-llama2-12.8m and 25.6m both carry an explicit `license:mit` metadata tag but no LICENSE file → 'probable' is the honest label (the project's own payloads/README.md independently records this exact caveat). AlignmentResearch/learned-planner: `license:apache-2.0` explicit metadata → VERIFIED (cleanest story). adamkarvonen/chess_llms: confirmed EMPTY metadata (only `region:us`, no license tag) → 'probable' via the verified-MIT training repo is correct; CONFIRM WITH AUTHOR before shipping. sander-wood/tunesformer: `license:mit` VERIFIED. The scouts' size math (params from real fp32 file size / 4, int4 = 0.5 B/param, +tokenizer +runtime) is consistent and matches the repo's established methodology.

LICENSE CAVEATS THAT TRAVEL WITH PICKS (the DOOM 'binary side A / GPL source side B' rule applies):
- Chess-GPT, delphi models, Othello-GPT, TinyCode-python: MIT/Apache by metadata tag, NO LICENSE file → PROBABLE. Permissive, but confirm before any commercial ship.
- Piper TTS: weights MIT VERIFIED, but the espeak-ng phoneme frontend is GPLv3 → ship its source side B or swap a permissive phonemizer.
- v86/KolibriOS/Buildroot-Linux/Bad-Apple-GB: engines/wrappers are BSD-2/Apache/ISC, but the OS kernels/ROMs are GPL-2.0 (+ LGPL-3.0 BIOS blobs) → source must accompany (on-brand, side B).
- Gutenberg corpora: PD-US VERIFIED (all pre-1928). KJV carries a UK Crown-copyright flag — safe for this US-based project, worth one line. Gutenberg trademark only restricts charging money.

DROPPED FROM HEADLINES (unknown license — moved to gaps): roneneldan/TinyStories-8M (no license), awonga/othello-gpt-7M (no license), edbeeching/decision_transformer_atari (no license + heavy emulator), huggan/crypto-gan (no license), Qwasm/LibreQuake (size+license unverified), simplewiki + TinyStories-dataset (CC-BY-SA / CDLA share-alike — copyleft, allowed only with license propagation, not 'permissive').

THE CHASE FRAMING: it's a clean medal ladder where the bottleneck is the BITRATE CLIMB, not the payload shelf. We have verified-permissive headliners for every rung 0.6→20 MB. The dream tape (20-40 MB) is double-unproven — the channel hasn't been demonstrated at those rates AND no clean-quality permissive model fits it; the only reliable dream payload is a scaled PD corpus. Acquire the five top_picks and the ladder is real from acoustic-now through the full electrical climb."
